trust_line: Start with facts you can check yourself — BOM, your state emergency service, and summers you already remember. That builds trust before any global score.

present.title: Strong El Niño risk into 2026–27
present.detail: Official US climate forecasts (NOAA/CPC, 9 Jul 2026) give about a 97% chance this El Niño lasts into early 2027 and about an 81% chance of a very strong peak late 2026 — among the strongest since 1950. For much of Australia that pattern often means hotter, drier weather and a harder fire season. Some computer models that track deep ocean heat look even more severe than soft media summaries.
present.verify: BOM climate outlooks for your state; CPC ENSO discussion for the global numbers.

present.title: Extreme heat already hit in early 2026
present.detail: Early 2026 brought extreme Australian heat, including reports near 50°C under a heat-dome pattern. Households, hospitals, and power systems had to cope in real time — not only in models.
present.verify: BOM extreme temperature records; your local council heatwave plan.

present.title: Bushfire readiness is seasonal work
present.detail: Dry fuels plus a strong El Niño path raise bushfire danger. Smoke can harm people far from the flames. If you live near bush or grassland, a written leave-early plan is normal prep — not drama.
present.verify: Your state fire service app (CFA, RFS, DFES, QFES, etc.) and fire danger ratings.

present.title: Water and farms already feel the seasons
present.detail: When outlooks turn dry, storages, stock, and irrigation decisions move first. Check local water authority notices and farm drought support pages for your region — not only national headlines.
present.verify: State water authority dam levels and rural assistance pages.

historic.title: Black Summer 2019–20
historic.detail: Still the national reference for how fire, smoke, and health systems can be stressed together. Most adults remember it — which is why it is useful for trust, not only history.
historic.verify: Royal Commission into National Natural Disaster Arrangements summaries.

historic.title: Past strong El Niño summers
historic.detail: Years like 1997–98 and 2015–16 class events are the practical analogues Australians use when BOM outlooks turn hot and dry. Strong El Niño does not guarantee identical weather everywhere, but it tilts the odds.
historic.verify: BOM ENSO wrap-ups and historical rainfall anomaly maps.

historic.title: Recent heatwave and fire seasons
historic.detail: Many communities already have a personal timeline of bad heat days, power stress, or smoke. That lived memory is part of the evidence base — not a substitute for official warnings, but a reason to take them seriously.
historic.verify: Your state health heat alerts and fire agency season reviews.

prep: Bookmark BOM warnings and install your state emergency / fire app
Heat plan: cool room, water, check on neighbours who live alone
If in a bushfire zone: written leave-early plan, go-bag, P2 masks for smoke
Know your local flood and fire maps if you are coastal or near bush
Small buffer of water, food, and medicine if you can afford it
evacuation_note: Evacuation routes and leave-early decisions are local. Use your state fire service advice, council maps, and the plan you write before a bad day — not a generic national webpage.
